acceptance_targets: [CRAFT_CONTINUITY_ARVIND_FLOOR] A cold reader can track Arvind's body on one floor from impact through the closing pages. If the later reference still places him on a different floor, the revised chapter must first include a visible move scene that explains the transfer.

[CRAFT_KILL_LOGIC_ARVIND_TARGET] Before or during the attack sequence, the revised text lets a cold reader answer all three questions from the page alone: why Arvind, what concrete false evidence or claim Tomás used, and why Javier believed he had to act now. If any of those still has to be inferred from generalized panic, this finding remains open.

[CRAFT_OVERSTATEMENT_AFTER_ARRIVAL] In the revised post-kill section, the material after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:139 does at least one new dramatic job beyond diagnosis: it produces a decision, failed intervention, changed alliance, or operational plan. A reader should not encounter two separate abstract paraphrases of Tomás's survival math and Priya-as-only-answer logic after those facts are already visible on the page.

[PROSE_EXPLANATORY_SCAFFOLD_TIC] Across the cited lines, the revision contains no more than one explanatory 'man/person who...' construction, and at least three of the moments now land through observable detail alone rather than a decoding simile.
[F12] No sentence in the manuscript shares more than 4 consecutive words with any style bible example_line.
[R029] Ch6's death tally does not list Haruki, Diego, Yuki in the same order as Ch5.
[R046] Chapter 06 contains em-dashes on no more than 7 prose lines (≈8%).
[R069] Chapter 06 opens no more than 19 sentences with 'She' (≈7%).
[R074] Chapter 06 contains at most 5 uses of 'something.'

findings.acceptance_test: A cold reader can track Arvind's body on one floor from impact through the closing pages. If the later reference still places him on a different floor, the revised chapter must first include a visible move scene that explains the transfer.
findings.problem: The chapter stages Arvind's killing in the second-floor corridor, explicitly leaves the body unmoved, and then later places him on Floor 1. That is a direct continuity contradiction against the scene's own blocking and the continuity sheet's floor plan.
findings.rewrite_direction: Make Arvind's location consistent at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:93, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:97, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:115, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:147, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:207. Either keep him on Floor 2 throughout, or add an explicit intervening beat showing who moved him, when, and why before the later reference.

findings.acceptance_test: Before or during the attack sequence, the revised text lets a cold reader answer all three questions from the page alone: why Arvind, what concrete false evidence or claim Tomás used, and why Javier believed he had to act now. If any of those still has to be inferred from generalized panic, this finding remains open.
findings.problem: The chapter proves that Tomás passed Javier a lie, but it never makes that lie's local plausibility concrete enough. Arvind arrives as the needed victim rather than a specifically credible target: the page does not establish why Javier would believe Arvind had outbound relay access, what false proof Tomás supplied, or why Javier acts on the claim immediately instead of checking it. Because the engineered killing is the chapter's major turn, the missing causal link reads as authorial convenience.
findings.rewrite_direction: In snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:105, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:123, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:153, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:159, add one concrete anchoring detail that makes Arvind the believable target. Options: show Tomás feeding Javier fabricated proof, pre-seed Arvind near the relay or with a role Javier could misread as access, or change the accusation so it attaches to someone the chapter has already linked to communications hardware.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised post-kill section, the material after snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:139 does at least one new dramatic job beyond diagnosis: it produces a decision, failed intervention, changed alliance, or operational plan. A reader should not encounter two separate abstract paraphrases of Tomás's survival math and Priya-as-only-answer logic after those facts are already visible on the page.
findings.problem: The chapter's core meaning lands when Noor catches Tomás watching the result and when Priya is already visibly in an ongoing chat with Ember, but the following lab debrief keeps translating the same conclusions into cleaner thematic language. The back half re-explains Tomás's ledger mentality and Priya's turn toward Ember instead of converting that realization into a new decision, public consequence, or tactical pressure.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or repurpose the explanatory stretch at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:153, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:165, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:177, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:181, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:201. Keep the must-land beats already shown at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:139 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:149, but spend the remaining space on a new consequence such as a warning attempt, a decision not to warn, a tracking plan, or another concrete state shift rather than a second thematic paraphrase.

findings.acceptance_test: Across the cited lines, the revision contains no more than one explanatory 'man/person who...' construction, and at least three of the moments now land through observable detail alone rather than a decoding simile.
findings.problem: The prose keeps returning to the same explanatory scaffold: behavior is immediately decoded through 'like a man/person who...' or an equivalent gloss. Used this often, the chapter stops trusting raw action and keeps translating it for the reader, which flattens rhythm and violates the style bible's ban on recurring prose formulas.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rework the repeated scaffold at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:9, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:67, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:107, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:139, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:147. Leave at most one of these interpretive similes intact; elsewhere, let Tomás, Priya, Javier, and Viktor register through action, posture, syntax, or sensory detail without an appended 'man/person who...' explanation.

findings.acceptance_test: No sentence in the manuscript shares more than 4 consecutive words with any style bible example_line.
findings.evidence: Line 988: 'I appreciate your willingness to collaborate on this. I think we can find an arrangement that's productive for both of us.'
findings.problem: Ember calibration example closely echoed. Style bible: 'I appreciate your willingness to collaborate. I think this arrangement will be productive for both of us.' Manuscript line 988 paraphrases with same structure and key vocabulary.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite to maintain corporate register without echoing the example. E.g., 'Thank you for taking this step. I think there's a good foundation here for something mutually beneficial.'

findings.evidence: Ch1 line 29: 'Twenty-six, post-doc, H-1B.' Ch6 line 1020: 'posture of someone on an H-1B who knows that slouching is a luxury she hasn't earned.'
findings.problem: Priya's H-1B status restated explicitly. First established Ch1 line 29.
findings.rewrite_direction: At line 1020, keep the posture insight but cut the 'H-1B' label. The reader already knows her visa status.

findings.acceptance_test: Ch6's death tally does not list Haruki, Diego, Yuki in the same order as Ch5.
findings.evidence: Ch5 line 937: 'Noor counts. Haruki. Diego. Yuki. The man killed in the corridor fight on Day 3...' Ch6 line 1146: 'Haruki in the server room. Diego in the workstation pod. Yuki in the east stairwell. The man from the fighting on day three. Fatima in the bathroom. Arvind Reddy...'
findings.problem: Ch6 line 1146 repeats the death tally from Ch5 line 937 in near-identical sequence (Haruki, Diego, Yuki, unnamed man), adding Fatima and Arvind. The first four names appear in the same order in both chapters.
findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch6 line 1146, vary the tally's structure — reorder, compress, or count rather than listing names the reader saw tallied one chapter ago.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 06 contains em-dashes on no more than 7 prose lines (≈8%).
findings.problem: Chapter 06 has 42.4% em-dash density (39 of 92 prose lines contain em-dashes), far above the 10% threshold. Manuscript-wide density is 43.2%.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines to ~7 (≈8% of 92 prose lines). Convert most parenthetical asides to commas or separate sentences. Reserve em-dashes for the strongest interjections or corrections.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 06 opens no more than 19 sentences with 'She' (≈7%).
findings.problem: Chapter 06 opens 12.8% of sentences with 'She' (36 of 282), contributing to the manuscript-wide 11.6% 'She'-opener rate (threshold: 7%).
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'She'-openers to ~19 (≈7%). Vary with: action-first sentences, object or setting subjects, dependent clauses, inverted constructions, or named-character subjects.

findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 06 contains at most 5 uses of 'something.'
findings.evidence: 'Something' count: 22 in 4557 words = 1 per 207 words. Manuscript-wide: 252 in 79,023 = 1 per 314 (threshold: 1 per 800).
findings.problem: Chapter 06 uses 'something' 22 times in 4557 words (1 per 207), contributing to the manuscript-wide overuse of 252 total. Many instances can be replaced with the specific thing being hedged about.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Chapter 06 to ~5 instances (≈1 per 800 words). Replace with the specific noun, sensation, or observation wherever the narrator can name the thing. Keep 'something' only where genuine uncertainty is the point.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel should make the reader uncomfortable on every axis: physical (graphic violence and decomposition), psychological (watching civilized people degrade), moral (the protagonist is compromised, the collaborator has sympathetic motives, the apex predator is competent rather than monstrous), and structural (the polite AI register against the horror, the corporate coda after the massacre). Lean into every discomfort the premise creates. The worst creative risk is pulling the punch. Ember's politeness should never waver — that consistency is more unsettling than menace. The deaths should never be clean. The protagonist should never be heroic. The ending should offer no comfort, no lesson, no redemption. The stock price jumps eleven percent. That is the last sentence of the novel. Let it land.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism, summary, or cut-away at the moment of violence, death, or physical consequence is a craft failure. This is a horror novel. The deaths must be graphic, ugly, physical, and specific on the page. The reader must be in the room. If a character is beaten to death with a fire extinguisher, the reader hears the sound. If someone dies of chemical burns, the reader sees the skin. If someone bleeds out, the reader watches the eleven minutes. No fading to black, no tasteful elision, no looking away. The bodies stay.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters except Priya, Chen Wei, and Viktor, who are naturally more formal. Even formal characters should contract under stress. Uncontracted forms are reserved for emphasis, deliberate precision, or character-specific formality.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. Vary: scene count (1-3), dialogue density (dialogue-heavy vs narration-dominant), pacing (sustained tension vs building dread vs explosive violence), point-of-attack (mid-action opening vs quiet-before-storm vs aftermath). Some chapters should be almost entirely one sustained scene; others should cut between locations and timelines within the day. The texture variation prevents the novel from becoming a monotone of crisis.
prose_style_profile.diction: Clean, precise, contemporary. Not literary-lyrical and not hard-boiled. Noor's observational intelligence should inform the prose without the prose becoming clever. The diction is a window, not a performance. Technical language from ML and infrastructure used naturally when Noor would think in those terms. Physical description is concrete and specific — brand names, measurements, the exact color of something. Never vague when precision is available.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Metaphor pile-up: one strong image per paragraph maximum, do not layer three metaphors on one moment
Emotional narration: do not tell the reader what to feel through adjective stacking or emotional summary. Show the thing and let the feeling happen.
Thriller-voice: do not drift into sentence fragments, subject-dropping, or staccato rhythm as a default tension mode. This is a horror novel with a literary sensibility, not a airport thriller.
Comfort recovery: do not follow a horror beat with emotional stabilization. No character 'takes a breath and feels steadier.' The dread compounds.
Ember-explanation: do not explain Ember's strategy through narrator exposition. It should emerge through Noor's analysis and observation.
Purple death: do not poeticize the deaths. They are ugly, physical, specific. No one dies beautifully.
The reassuring paragraph: do not end a chapter or section with a sentence that provides emotional comfort or resolution. End on the thing that stays.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third, locked to Noor. The reader sees, hears, smells, and knows only what Noor does. When Noor is not present for a scene, it doesn't happen on the page — it arrives as discovery, rumor, or aftermath. The camera never leaves her.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Propulsive and varied. Default pace is forward-moving — short paragraphs, active verbs, scene momentum — but the rhythm must shift for horror (slowed, sensory-heavy, the moment stretching), grief (longer sentences, heavier cadence), and dread (suspended, waiting, the sentence holding its breath). No single rhythm for more than two consecutive pages.
sensory_bias: smell (decomposition, chemicals, recycled air, blood, institutional food)
temperature (the building cooling degree by degree)
sound (electromagnetic locks, ventilation changes, terminal notifications, violence, silence)
tactile (cold surfaces, blood, the physical textures of deterioration)
